faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: The ten things first-time users ask. Each answer links to the page that handles it.

firstPet.q: Which species should a first-time keeper pick?
firstPet.a: Pick one with forgiving temperature/humidity needs and gentle handling. Leopard gecko, crested gecko, corn snake and bearded dragon are typical. Run the 8-question match quiz for a personalized top-3.

wildlifeLaw.q: Who has to file under the wildlife law?
wildlifeLaw.a: Effective 2025-12-14, owners of mammals, birds, reptiles and amphibians must file with the Ministry of Environment when keeping, transferring or losing the animal. White-list trades also require filings.

insurance.q: Can reptiles or amphibians get pet insurance?
insurance.a: Korean pet insurance is dog/cat only. Reptiles, amphibians and arthropods almost never qualify. Pettography recommends building an emergency reserve instead.

morphCost.a: Genetic rarity, combos (multiple morphs), and het/homozygous all matter. Korean baselines: common morphs ₩30–80k, mid ₩100–300k, premium ₩500k–1.5M.

enclosureSetup.q: How much does first-month setup cost?
enclosureSetup.a: Depends on category and tier. Reptile basic setup runs ~₩200–400k; premium ~₩600k–1M. The setup estimator gives a per-part breakdown.

feeding.q: Where do I buy feeder food, and how do I store it?
feeding.a: Crickets, dubia roaches, mealworms, superworms, pinky mice, CGD all differ by species. Order from KR shops (The Zoo, Newrun, Mimine Aqua, etc.); keep live insects in ventilated boxes and order only enough for 3–5 days.

emergency.q: What do I do in an emergency?
emergency.a: Each species has its own warning signs (refusal to eat, bad shed, trauma, heat shock). The /sos page gives a 1-minute category checklist and a 1-tap call to the nearest 24h exotic vet.

multiPet.a: The current build assumes one pet at a time. Multi-pet is on the roadmap; in the meantime, use /backup to keep separate JSON snapshots per animal.

dataLoss.q: Will I lose my data if I clear the browser?
dataLoss.a: Yes — everything is stored only in this browser's localStorage. Export regularly from /backup so you can restore on another device or after a browser wipe.

mapKey.a: The map renders only when VITE_KAKAO_MAP_KEY is set in your build environment. Self-hosters can grab a JavaScript key from the Kakao Developers Console.

about.title: One rare pet's whole life in one app
about.lead: Pettography curates every stage of a reptile/arthropod/exotic bird/amphibian/small mammal keeper's journey, from first pick to farewell, in a single Korean-context app.
about.whyBody: In a Korean pet market built around dogs and cats, owners of rare pets juggle scattered information. Major apps focus on dog/cat. Wildlife filing duties live buried on environment ministry sites. Pettography aims to be both the first tool you open in an emergency and the lightweight diary you check 2-3x a week.

location.title: Location and distance are first-class
location.body: All location data ships with distance, and switching location is one chip away.

emergency.title: Emergencies get priority
emergency.body: 24h vets, critical supply levels and environment range breaches lead visually and in sort order.

sources.title: Don't hide sources
sources.body: Mock data, external links, government registration status are all surfaced. Trust comes from metadata, not raw data.

diaryVsMedical.title: Light on diary, deep on medical
diaryVsMedical.body: Diary and routine are fast-entry; medical, habitat and budget pages carry rich context per screen.

beyondCards.title: Beyond identical cards
beyondCards.body: We avoid endless grids of the same card. Information weight drives the affordance choice.

legend.title: Genetics quick reference
legend.body: A het (heterozygous) animal carries one recessive copy: it looks normal but passes the trait on. Co-dominant genes show a base morph with one copy and a distinct 'super' form with two. Dominant traits show with a single copy. A '66% het' tag is the chance a normal-looking sibling is a carrier.

feeding.title: Feeding scheduler & prey-size calculator
feeding.subtitle: Work out the right prey size and feeding frequency for your pet's species and stage, then log feedings to predict the next one and catch refusal streaks early. All data stays in this browser.
feeding.disclaimer: These values are general guidance. Adjust to the individual animal's build, appetite, and health, and to your vet or an experienced keeper's advice.

preyRules.betweenEyes: Offer prey no larger than the space between the eyes.
preyRules.bodyWeightPct: Prey weight should be about 10–15% of body weight.
preyRules.girthMatch: Offer prey about the same as the widest girth (roughly 1–1.25×).
preyRules.bodyLength: Offer prey about the same length as the body, or smaller.
preyRules.greensPlusInsects: Daily greens with insects sized to the animal's stage.

ball-python.note: For ball pythons, pick a rodent about the size of the snake's widest girth. They're prone to obesity, so don't overfeed, and avoid handling for 24–48 hours after a meal.

corn-snake.note: For corn snakes, match the mouse to the body's girth. Every 7 days while growing and 10–14 days as an adult is typical.

leopard-gecko.note: For leopard geckos, offer insects smaller than the gap between the eyes. Juveniles eat 5–7 insects daily; adults 2–3× a week, with calcium/vitamin dusting.

crested-gecko.note: Crested geckos do best on a complete diet (CGD), with insects smaller than the eye gap as a 1–2× weekly supplement.

bearded-dragon.note: Bearded dragons eat more insects as juveniles and shift to mostly greens as adults. Keep insects smaller than the gap between the eyes.

mexican-redknee-tarantula.note: For the Mexican redknee, offer prey at or below its body length. Adults are fine every 1–2 weeks, and they fast during pre-molt, so remove uneaten prey.

emperor-scorpion.note: Feed emperor scorpions prey sized to their body about once a week. Pause feeding around molts and keep humidity stable.

pacman-frog.note: For Pacman frogs, offer prey smaller than the gap between the eyes. They obese easily, so space adult meals to every 4–7 days.

axolotl.note: Axolotls live on earthworms and pellets. Feed juveniles daily and adults 2–3× a week, only what they finish, removing leftovers to protect water quality.

fallback.reptile: Prey size and frequency vary a lot between reptiles. Use the gap between the eyes as a baseline and check a species-specific source.
fallback.arthropod: Offer arthropods prey sized to their body, and expect fasting before a molt. Check a care sheet for your species.
fallback.amphibian: Offer amphibians prey smaller than the gap between the eyes. They obese easily, so manage portion size and frequency.
fallback.bird: Give birds species-appropriate seed/pellets and fresh greens daily. Avoid toxic foods such as avocado.
fallback.mammal: Give small mammals a species-appropriate diet and fresh vegetables daily. Nutritional needs vary, so check a dedicated source.

assistant.title: Care Assistant
assistant.subtitle: Pick a symptom and get rule-based triage with clear next steps. Advice is tuned to your active pet's category.
assistant.disclaimer: This is rule-based guidance. It does not replace a veterinarian. If anything seems off, contact a clinic.
assistant.context: Guidance is shown for {{name}} ({{category}}). Advice is tuned to this category's needs.
assistant.contextGeneric: No active pet is set, so general guidance is shown. Pick a species in onboarding to tune advice to its category.
assistant.step1Hint: Choose the one that fits best.
assistant.reminder: If anything seems off, don't hesitate — contact a clinic.
assistant.emptyDescription: Choose a symptom above and we'll show guidance after one or two quick questions.
assistant.emergencyBody: Begin emergency care without delay and contact a clinic. Time matters.

options.recent: A few days, activity is about normal
options.weightLoss: Refusing food with visible weight or body loss

options.stuckPatches: Patches of skin stuck and not coming off
options.toesOrEyes: Stuck shed constricting toes, tail tip, or eyes

options.mildWheeze: Occasional wheeze but active as usual

options.notMoving: Barely moving and slow to react
options.unresponsive: Limp and barely responds to touch

injury.label: Injury or bleeding
injury.question: What does the wound look like?

options.burnOrHeatStress: Burn marks or panting from overheating

options.newRoutine: Started after a recent change in setup or routine
options.hiding: Hiding constantly and not behaving normally
options.aggressionOrTremor: Sudden aggression, tremors, or abnormal posture

advice.anorexiaRecent: Short-term food refusal is common with environment change, stress, or seasonal shifts. Keep the enclosure stable and observe for a few more days. Always provide water and log weight regularly.
advice.anorexiaWeeks: Refusal lasting weeks calls for checking low temperatures, parasites, and environmental stress. Verify temp, humidity, and heating first; if there's no improvement, consult a vet.
advice.anorexiaWeightLoss: Refusal with weight loss may mean dehydration or metabolic disease and needs prompt care. Don't force-feed; keep the animal warm and contact a clinic.
advice.sheddingInProgress: This is a normal shed. Don't handle the animal and keep humidity in range. A hide and rough surfaces help the shed come off.
advice.sheddingStuckPatches: For stuck shed, raise humidity and assist with lukewarm misting or a humid hide. Never peel it off by force; if it doesn't clear in a few days, consult a vet.
advice.sheddingToesOrEyes: Stuck shed on toes, tail tip, or eyes can cut off blood flow and cause necrosis or blindness. If a gentle lukewarm soak doesn't release it, contact a clinic immediately.
advice.respiratoryMildWheeze: A mild wheeze can come from low temperature, dry air, or dust. Raise heat into range and check substrate dust. If it worsens, see a vet.
advice.respiratoryOpenMouth: Open-mouth breathing is a sign of severe respiratory distress. Keep heating stable and contact a clinic without delay.
advice.respiratoryMucusBubbles: Nasal discharge, bubbles, or mucus strongly suggest a respiratory infection. Keep the animal warm and seek care immediately.
advice.lethargySlightlyQuiet: Mild lethargy can be normal rest, digestion, or temperature shifts. Check heating and night-time temperature drop, and observe. Also watch appetite and stool.
advice.lethargyNotMoving: If it's slow to react and barely moving, check for low temperature, dehydration, and infection. Correct temp and humidity; if there's no improvement, see a vet.
advice.lethargyUnresponsive: Limp and barely responsive is an emergency. Keep the animal warm and contact a clinic immediately.
advice.injuryMinorScrape: Gently rinse a minor scrape with clean water, keep the enclosure clean, and observe. If it swells or oozes, see a vet.
advice.injuryActiveBleeding: Bleeding that won't stop is an emergency. Apply gentle pressure with clean gauze to slow it while you contact a clinic immediately.
advice.injuryProlapse: A prolapse (protruding organ or cloaca) is an emergency. Keep the exposed tissue clean and moist (e.g., gauze dampened with sugar water) and head to a clinic immediately.
advice.tempHumiditySlightlyOff: If readings are slightly off, adjust with heating, misting, or ventilation. Use two digital gauges to check the hot spot and cool zone together.
advice.tempHumidityWayOff: Readings well outside range affect appetite, immunity, and shedding. Check the thermostat and heating or humidity gear to normalize quickly, and watch the animal's condition.
advice.tempHumidityBurnOrHeatStress: A burn or heat stress is an emergency. Turn off the heat source at once, move the animal somewhere cool, then contact a clinic. Always guard heat lamps with a cage and thermostat.
advice.behaviorNewRoutine: A behavior change right after a setup or routine change may be adjustment. Keep the enclosure stable, reduce disturbance, and observe for a few days.
advice.behaviorHiding: Constant hiding can signal temperature discomfort, stress, or pain. Check temp, humidity, and hide placement; if appetite also drops, see a vet.
advice.behaviorAggressionOrTremor: Sudden aggression, tremors, or abnormal posture can mean pain or a neurological or metabolic problem. Check the environment and consult a vet as soon as possible.

categoryNotes.anorexiaArthropod: Arthropods naturally refuse food before a molt (pre-molt). A dark, plump abdomen can signal an upcoming molt — leave it undisturbed and wait.
categoryNotes.anorexiaReptile: Reptile anorexia often overlaps with low temperatures or brumation. Check the hot spot and cool zone temperatures first.
categoryNotes.anorexiaAmphibian: Amphibians are sensitive to water quality and temperature, so refusal is common. Check water temperature, cleanliness, and dechlorination first.
categoryNotes.sheddingArthropod: Molting is the most vulnerable time for arthropods. Never handle during a molt, remove feeders, and just keep humidity stable.
categoryNotes.sheddingReptile: Reptile shedding trouble is usually caused by low humidity. Assist with a humid hide and misting.
categoryNotes.respiratoryAmphibian: Amphibians rely heavily on skin respiration, so water quality and humidity directly affect breathing. Check water cleanliness and ventilation together.
categoryNotes.respiratoryBird: Birds have very sensitive airways and deteriorate fast. If you see tail bobbing or open-mouth breathing, seek care without delay.
categoryNotes.lethargyReptile: Reptiles are ectotherms, so low temperatures make them look lethargic. Before a vet visit, bring heating back into the normal range.
categoryNotes.lethargyArthropod: Arthropods can move much less around a molt. Check whether a molt happened recently first.
categoryNotes.tempHumidityAmphibian: Amphibians are especially vulnerable to dryness and heat. Keep humidity high and avoid direct sun and overheating.
categoryNotes.tempHumidityReptile: For reptiles, the temperature gradient between hot spot and cool zone is key. Measure both separately to maintain the gradient.

brumation.title: Brumation Planner
brumation.subtitle: Plan a reptile's brumation safely, phase by phase — for bearded dragons, some geckos, tortoises and more. From the vet check through fasting, a gradual temperature step-down, dormancy, gradual warm-up and recovery.
brumation.disclaimer: The phase durations shown are defaults based on common keeper references, not precise prescriptions. Real timing varies with species, individual and health, and should always be decided with your veterinarian.

safety.title: Read this before you start
safety.intro: Brumation is a risky process that can be life-threatening if done carelessly. Confirm every condition below first.
safety.healthyOnly: Only attempt this with healthy adults. Never brumate a sick, underweight or recovering animal.
safety.vetFirst: A pre-brumation vet check is essential. Parasites, infections or mouth rot can worsen dangerously during dormancy.
safety.noJuvenile: Do not brumate juveniles. Only fully grown animals at a stable weight are candidates.
safety.fastBeforeCool: Clear the gut with a full fast before cooling. Undigested food can rot at low temperatures and cause sepsis.
safety.monitorWeight: Weigh the animal regularly throughout, and stop brumation immediately if you see a sharp drop or any warning signs.
safety.tempControl: Stable cool temperatures are critical. Use a thermometer to keep the species' recommended range at all times.

notReptile.title: Brumation mainly applies to reptiles
notReptile.desc: Your active pet is not a reptile. Brumation applies to most reptiles (and a few other species), but you can still build a plan here for reference.

vetCheck.title: Vet Check
vetCheck.desc: A pre-brumation health check to confirm the animal is fit to brumate. Review weight, parasites and infections, and only proceed if all is clear.

fasting.title: Fasting (gut clearing)
fasting.desc: Stop feeding and let the gut empty completely. Keep temperatures normal for now so any remaining food is fully digested and passed.

coolDown.title: Gradual Cool-down
coolDown.desc: Step the enclosure temperature down to the target over several days, and gradually shorten the photoperiod (light hours) too.

dormancy.title: Dormancy (the cool period)
dormancy.desc: The core dormant stretch, holding the target cool temperature steadily. Minimise disturbance, but provide fresh water and check weight and condition periodically.

warmUp.title: Gradual Warm-up
warmUp.desc: Bring temperature and photoperiod back to normal husbandry over several days. Avoid warming up abruptly.

recovery.title: Recovery & refeeding
recovery.desc: Once back at normal temperatures, offer water first, then resume feeding with small portions. Watch for normal droppings and appetite returning.

form.title: Create a plan
form.intro: Set a start date and the length of each phase, and the full schedule and per-phase dates are calculated for you.

list.title: Saved plans
list.emptyDesc: Set a start date and phase durations above to create your first plan.

gear.uvbAlertTitle: Replace UVB bulbs even while they still light up
gear.uvbAlertBody: A UVB bulb's UV output fades long before its visible light does. It can still glow while emitting almost no UV, so replace it on schedule even if it looks fine. Skipping this is a leading cause of metabolic bone disease (MBD).

senior.title: Senior care
senior.subtitle: As your years together add up, care needs to change too. Gauge when your companion is entering its senior years and adjust to a gentler, more comfortable setup.

stageBlurb.juvenile: A time of rapid growth. Focus on nutrition and growth tracking to build a healthy foundation.
stageBlurb.adult: The most stable phase. Keep up your good routines and log baseline behavior so you can spot changes early.
stageBlurb.senior: Your companion is entering its senior years. Watch for small shifts in activity, appetite, and weight, and ease the habitat into something more comfortable.
stageBlurb.geriatric: These are precious years beyond the typical lifespan. Prioritize stability and comfort over big changes, and check in with your vet more often.

metric.estimateNote: Remaining time is a rough estimate based on the species' average lifespan. It can vary widely with each animal's health and environment.

form.title: Age details
form.ageHelper: An approximate value is fine if you're not sure. E.g. 4.5
form.acquiredAsHelper: Life stage when you got the animal. Helps estimate age when the exact birth date is unknown.
form.notesPlaceholder: Jot down recent condition, appetite, or behavior changes.

senior.tipsLeadSenior: Now that the senior years have begun, work the items below into the habitat and daily routine.
senior.tipsLeadEarly: Not a senior yet, but here are species-specific care points worth knowing in advance.

tips.reptileLowerBasking: Lower the basking spot and place it for easy climbing to reduce the risk of falls.
tips.reptileEasyDecor: Switch hides and steps to lower, more stable options to ease movement.
tips.reptileVetChecks: Schedule vet checkups more often to monitor organ and metabolic health.
tips.reptileWeightAppetite: Log weight and appetite changes more frequently to catch warning signs early.
tips.reptileJointOrgan: Watch for age-related changes like joint stiffness and kidney or liver decline.
tips.reptileHydration: Pay extra attention to hydration and humidity, as older animals tire of dehydration sooner.
tips.arthropodGentleHandling: Minimize handling and be especially gentle when you do.
tips.arthropodLowerClimb: Lower the enclosure height to reduce the risk of fatal falls and abdominal rupture.
tips.arthropodMoltWatch: Molts can be risky for older animals, so watch closely before and after a molt.
tips.arthropodStableHumidity: Keep temperature and humidity steady to minimize stress.
tips.birdPerchVariety: Offer perches of varying thickness plus lower perches to ease strain on feet and joints.
tips.birdSoftDiet: Increase softer, easier-to-eat foods and keep the diet balanced.
tips.birdWarmth: Provide extra warmth as temperature regulation becomes harder.
tips.birdVetChecks: See an avian vet more often to screen for hidden illness.
tips.birdWeightAppetite: Check weight and appetite often to catch changes quickly.
tips.birdGentleRoutine: Reduce changes and stimulation, keeping a calm, stable routine.
tips.amphibianWaterQuality: Pay closer attention to water quality and filtration to guard against weakened immunity.
tips.amphibianCoolerTemps: Keep water slightly cooler and stable to ease metabolic load.
tips.amphibianSkinWatch: Inspect the skin often for wounds or signs of infection.
tips.amphibianGentleHandling: Animals grow more sensitive, so minimize handling and make changes slowly.
tips.mammalSoftBedding: Use soft bedding to ease strain on joints and prevent pressure sores.
tips.mammalRamps: Add ramps and low steps to make climbing easier.
tips.mammalSoftDiet: Adjust to softer, easier-to-digest food to match dental condition.
tips.mammalVetChecks: Schedule checkups more often to monitor teeth, joints, and organs.
tips.mammalWeightAppetite: Log weight and appetite changes often to notice problems early.
tips.mammalDentalJoint: Watch for age-related issues like worn teeth and arthritis.

vitals.title: Vitals Counter
vitals.subtitle: Respiratory and heart rate are key health signals for exotic pets. Tap to count, compute beats-or-breaths per minute, and track the trend.

counter.title: Tap counter
counter.hint: Pick respiration or heart rate, set a window, press start, then tap once for every breath (or beat).
counter.notePlaceholder: Note the context (e.g. at rest, enclosure 28C)

guidance.title: Resting respiratory rate by category
guidance.reptile: Reptiles breathe roughly 10-40 per minute at rest, varying widely by species. It rises with temperature and stress.
guidance.amphibian: Amphibians breathe partly through the skin, so visible breathing is irregular. At rest it is roughly 20-50 per minute, varying a lot by species and water temperature.
guidance.bird: Birds have a fast metabolism, so even at rest it is roughly 25-50+ per minute, faster in smaller species.
guidance.mammal: Small mammals have a fast metabolism, roughly 30-100+ per minute at rest, varying widely by species and size.
guidance.arthropod: Arthropods have no lungs, so a typical breathing count does not apply. Watch activity and posture changes instead.
guidance.unknown: Register a species to see approximate resting respiratory-rate guidance for its category.
guidance.caveat: These are all very approximate and vary a lot with temperature, species, and individual. Treat them as a tracking reference, not a diagnosis.
guidance.vet: If breathing looks different from usual or is open-mouthed and labored, contact your vet right away.

history.title: Reading history
history.emptyDesc: Save your first reading with the tap counter and the trend will build up here.

cohab.title: Cohabitation Checker
cohab.subtitle: Get a clear, rule-based verdict on whether two species can share an enclosure. Most exotic pets are meant to be kept alone, and this tool explains why.
cohab.empty: Pick two species to see a cohabitation verdict.
cohab.disclaimer: This verdict is general guidance based on common husbandry principles and does not account for every individual situation. If you are considering cohabitation, always pair it with ample space, quarantine, and advice from an expert or vet. When in doubt, keeping animals solo is safest.

principle.title: Most exotic pets are meant to be kept alone
principle.body: Nearly all reptiles, arthropods, and amphibians are naturally solitary; housing them together causes chronic stress, territorial fighting, cannibalism, and disease spread. "Looking lonely" is a human feeling — cohabitation rarely benefits the animal.

picker.title: Choose two species to check
picker.intro: Pick the two species to compare. Species A defaults to your active pet. Choose the same species twice to check same-species cohabitation.

headline.sameSpeciesOnly: Never mix species — same species only

summary.no: This pairing is not recommended. Sharing one enclosure brings a high risk of stress, aggression, predation, and disease spread. Keep each animal solo in its own enclosure.
summary.caution: This species should be kept alone. Even with the same species, sharing a space easily leads to territorial disputes and stress, so keep them separate unless you have a specific purpose such as breeding.
summary.sameSpeciesOnly: This species is social enough to be kept in same-species groups. However, never mix it with other species, and provide ample space and an appropriate sex ratio.
summary.ok: This is a social animal that risks depression and self-harm when kept alone, so keeping several of the same species together is recommended. Provide ample space and a proper introduction process.

reasons.mixSpeciesNever: Never house different species together — there is virtually no safe cross-species combination.
reasons.territorial: A territorial animal, so sharing a space leads to disputes and aggression.
reasons.cannibalism: Risk of cannibalism — one animal may eat the other.
reasons.sizeMismatch: A size difference means the smaller one can be injured or become prey.
reasons.diseaseRisk: Parasites, bacteria, and viruses spread quickly between animals.
reasons.differentNeeds: Different temperature, humidity, and diet needs mean one animal is always stressed.
reasons.stress: Simply being housed together builds chronic stress, leading to food refusal and weakened immunity.
reasons.predation: A predator–prey relationship in which one may eat the other.
reasons.socialNeedsGroup: A social animal that risks loneliness, depression, and self-harm when kept alone.
reasons.sameSpeciesGroupOk: Group housing is fine among the same species (but not with other species).
reasons.spaceRequired: Needs an enclosure large enough, with enough hides, for multiple animals.
reasons.sexRatio: Manage the sex ratio carefully (e.g. same-sex groups) to prevent fighting and overbreeding.
reasons.soloMandatory: A species that must be kept alone — one animal per enclosure.

guide.title: Solitary-keeping basics
guide.solitaryDefault: Unless a species is clearly known to be social, the default is always to keep it alone.
guide.noIntroduce: Don't add a "friend" because an animal looks lonely — for most species, cohabitation is a threat.
guide.stressHidden: Exotics hide stress well. By the time you see food refusal, hiding, or weight loss, things may already be serious.
guide.quarantine: Quarantine any newly acquired animal separately for at least 30–90 days to prevent disease spread.
guide.oneEnclosure: One animal per enclosure is safest — it removes competition for space, hides, and food entirely.

social.title: Social species (the exceptions)
social.intro: The species below are exceptions that can or should be kept in same-species groups. Even so, never mix them with other species.

notes.budgerigar: Best kept in groups — they get very lonely alone. Needs a roomy cage and flight space.
notes.cockatiel: Highly social; can be kept in pairs or groups — provide ample space and interaction time.
notes.sugar-glider: Never keep alone — at least two same-sex animals required. Isolation causes depression and self-harm.
notes.madagascar-hissing-cockroach: A social, colony-living arthropod — group housing among the same species is natural.

wishlist.subtitle: Collect species you hope to keep one day, and check your readiness before bringing one home.
wishlist.responsibleNote: Fill in your readiness before bringing one home. A prepared adoption — not an impulse — protects the animal.
wishlist.experienceCaution: This is an advanced species. Build up comparable experience before taking it on.

taming.title: Taming
taming.subtitle: Handling is for the animal's tolerance, not the keeper's wish. Log sessions slowly and briefly, and watch stress signals and acclimation over time.

low.title: This species has low handling tolerance
low.body: Keep it minimal and brief. Handle only when truly needed (health checks, cleaning, moving) — observation is usually enough. Not handling can be the best care.

medium.title: This species has moderate handling tolerance
medium.body: Short, calm, regular sessions can build trust over time. Stop and return the animal as soon as you see stress signals.

high.title: This species tolerates handling well
high.body: It generally handles well, but still put the animal's signals first. It's fine to skip a session on an off day.

form.title: Log a session
form.durationHelp: Time you actually handled the animal
form.stressSignsHelp: Select every behavior shown this session. Leave empty if none.
form.notePlaceholder: Note the context, environment, or anything notable.

guidance.noData: No sessions yet. Start calmly with a short session.
guidance.lowTolerance: This species has low handling tolerance. Keep frequency and duration minimal, and rely mostly on observation.
guidance.highStress: Recent sessions show a lot of stress signals. Cut back on sessions and focus on environmental acclimation.
guidance.goodProgress: Calmness is improving. Keep this pace and continue slowly.
guidance.declining: Calmness is dropping. Shorten your sessions and pay closer attention to the animal's signals.
guidance.steady: Acclimation is steady. Keep up these short, gentle sessions.

empty.title: No handling sessions yet
empty.desc: Log your first short session. Over time you'll see the acclimation trend.

kit.title: Emergency kit
kit.subtitle: Power outages, heatwaves, sudden illness. In a crisis, minutes matter. Prepare in advance so you never have to scramble.

readiness.title: Readiness
readiness.desc: Check off items to build up your household's emergency readiness.

hints.generic: Register a pet and we'll show a preparedness tip for its species. Start with warmth, transport, and contacts.
hints.reptile: For reptiles, warmth matters most during an outage. Keep heat packs and a power bank on hand, and check the enclosure often with a spare thermometer.
hints.amphibian: For amphibians, water quality and oxygen are vital. Filtration and aeration stop during an outage, so store clean water ahead of time and minimize temperature swings.
hints.arthropod: For arthropods, holding humidity and temperature is key. Keep misting water plus heat and ice packs ready so the enclosure doesn't swing suddenly.
hints.bird: Birds are sensitive to sudden temperature changes and stress. Keep an insulatable carrier with a cover, and prepare a heat backup for outages.
hints.mammal: Small mammals struggle to regulate body temperature. Prepare for both heat and cold with insulation, an ice pack, and plenty of water.

supplements.title: Supplement Dusting
supplements.subtitle: Track calcium, D3, and multivitamin dusting to prevent metabolic bone disease (MBD).
supplements.uvbHint: Dusting frequency depends on UVB exposure and staple diet. With ample UVB, reduce D3 supplementation.
supplements.disclaimer: This schedule is general guidance. The right plan depends on the individual's age, UVB, and staple diet; consult an exotics vet if you see warning signs.

mbd.title: MBD prevention
mbd.body: Calcium and UVB deficiency are the leading causes of metabolic bone disease (MBD). Don't skip calcium dusting for insectivorous and herbivorous reptiles.

gutLoad.title: Gut-load over dusting
gutLoad.body: This group is generally not dusted. Deliver nutrition by gut-loading feeder insects with nutritious feed for 24-48 hours instead.

guidance.reptile: Insectivorous reptiles (leopard geckos, juvenile bearded dragons) typically get plain calcium (no D3) on most feedings, calcium+D3 once or twice a week, and a multivitamin about weekly. With ample UVB, cut back on D3.
guidance.amphibian: Amphibians (such as pacman frogs) get calcium on most feedings and a multivitamin roughly weekly. Avoid over-supplementing.
guidance.arthropod: Arthropods like tarantulas and scorpions are generally not dusted. Deliver nutrition by gut-loading their feeders.
guidance.bird: Birds do best on a balanced pellet and vegetable diet. Keep calcium (e.g. cuttlebone) as a supplement and avoid over-supplementation.
guidance.mammal: Small mammals rely on a species-appropriate balanced diet. Supplement only on a vet's advice.
guidance.unknown: Register a pet first and we'll show dusting guidance matched to its species group.

feeders.title: Feeders
feeders.subtitle: Track your own feeder-insect cultures and bioactive cleanup crews so they stay healthy and well gut-loaded.
feeders.gutLoadNote: Home-cultured feeders are only as nutritious as what you gut-load them with — feed the feeders veggies, grains, or a dedicated chow on a regular cycle to keep your pets healthy.

form.title: Add a colony
form.name: Colony name
form.estimateHelper: Leave blank to record as unknown.
form.notesPlaceholder: Bin size, food type, grow-out plans…

care.dubia: Quiet, near odorless and high in protein. Gut-load with veggies and grain meal, and keep the bin well ventilated.
care.cricket: Noisy and smelly with fast breeding. Gut-load with fresh greens and a calcium chow, and remove dead ones promptly.
care.mealworm: Easy to keep but high in chitin, so better as a treat than a staple. Add veggie chunks to wheat bran for moisture and nutrition.
care.superworm: A large, protein-rich feeder. To pupate them, isolate each one individually like a pinhead.
care.isopod: A bioactive cleanup crew. Offer leaf litter, wood and a calcium source, with both a damp and a dry zone.
care.springtail: A bioactive cleanup crew that holds back mold. Keep it consistently moist and feed a pinch of rice or yeast.
care.other: Confirm the right food, temperature and humidity for your species and gut-load on a regular cycle.

productivity.dubia: Slow breeders — expect about 4–6 months to a steady, productive colony.
productivity.cricket: Fast breeders — a new generation in roughly 6–8 weeks.
productivity.mealworm: New larvae appear about 8–10 weeks after the beetles emerge.
productivity.superworm: Around 10–12 weeks through isolation, pupation and adults before they lay.
productivity.isopod: About 3–6 months for the culture to establish and become harvestable.
productivity.springtail: Booms quickly — about 4–6 weeks under the right conditions.
productivity.other: Productivity timing varies by species, so log how the head-count changes.

transport.title: Transport planner
transport.subtitle: Prepare for vet visits, moves, and expos safely. A secure container, steady temperature, and low stress are what matter most.
transport.disclaimer: This checklist is guidance to help you travel safely. Adapt it to your species and individual animal, and consult your vet when in doubt.

items.tempControl: Temperature control — heat or cool pack

tips.reptile: For ectotherms, keeping warmth steady in transit matters most. Wrap heat packs in cloth so they never touch the animal, and avoid direct sunlight entirely.
tips.amphibian: Amphibians dry out easily. Hold humidity with damp paper towel or sphagnum moss and keep moisture from drying out.
tips.arthropod: Arthropods are sensitive to shock and temperature swings. Minimize jostling while keeping ventilation and a stable temperature.
tips.bird: Birds stress easily. Cover the carrier dark to settle them and avoid sudden temperature changes.
tips.mammal: Keep small mammals warm and ventilated, and add familiar bedding or cloth to reduce stress.
tips.generic: Build every trip around three basics: a secure carrier, steady temperature, and minimal stress.

bioactive.body: In a bioactive setup the cleanup crew and plants break down waste. Keeping up with spot cleaning while minimizing full cleans and substrate swaps keeps the colony stable. The intervals below assume a standard setup, so adjust them to your environment.

safety.title: Safety audit
safety.subtitle: A guided enclosure audit to prevent escapes, burns, shocks, drowning, and injury before they happen. Prevention is everything.
safety.creed: Accidents are all about prevention — work through each item and remove the risk in advance.
safety.risksIntro: Accident types that are especially common for your active pet's category.
safety.risksAlertTitle: Watch these closely for this category
safety.disclaimer: This checklist is a general husbandry-safety guide and does not cover every hazard. Add your own checks for your species and individual animal.

score.title: Safety score
score.desc: Shows your enclosure's overall safety level from the share of items you've confirmed.

checklist.title: Safety checklist
checklist.intro: Confirm and fix each item yourself, then switch it on.

risks.generic: Escapes and burns are the most common accidents. Start with the lid and heat sources.
risks.heightEscape: Arboreal species escape using height — secure the top cover firmly.
risks.heatBurn: Direct contact with lamps or heaters causes burns; you need a guard and a thermostat.
risks.gapEscape: Even tiny gaps let them out — check every seam and the ventilation mesh.
risks.waterDepth: Amphibians can drown even in shallow water; manage depth and an escape ramp.
risks.toxins: Sensitive skin can absorb detergent or paint residue — use safe materials only.
risks.escapeRamp: Give animals that fall into bowls or water a ramp or foothold to climb out.
risks.fallHeight: A fall from height can be fatal for arthropods — keep the enclosure low.
risks.abdomenRupture: A fall can rupture a tarantula's abdomen; limit the climbing height.
risks.toxicFumes: Birds have very fragile airways — smoke and sprays can be fatal. Check ventilation.
risks.teflon: Overheated Teflon (PTFE) cookware is fatal to birds; keep them away from the kitchen.
risks.barSpacing: Wide bar spacing can trap heads or limbs, or allow escape — check the gap.
risks.chewedCords: Gnawing small mammals can bite cords and get shocked; shield all wiring.

seasonal.title: Seasonal care guide
seasonal.subtitle: Husbandry shifts with the seasons. Check this season's priorities for your pet's category, then preview all four seasons ahead. (Korea / northern-hemisphere timing.)
seasonal.disclaimer: General guidance for the northern hemisphere (Korea / Japan). Exact timing and temperatures vary by species, individual, and region, so cross-check your species' references and a vet's advice. Covers {{count}} seasons.

picker.label: Pick a category to see its seasonal tips

spring.name: Spring
spring.blurb: Activity and appetite return and the breeding season begins. Day-night swings are wide, so keep night heating on while gradually raising daytime temperatures.

summer.name: Summer
summer.blurb: Overheating and excess humidity are the biggest risks. Check the enclosure's temperature ceiling, avoid direct AC drafts, and ensure ample water and airflow.

autumn.name: Autumn
autumn.blurb: Shortening daylight shifts appetite and activity. Brumating species begin their prep; for others it's time to switch over to winter heating.

winter.name: Winter
winter.blurb: Heating and dryness are the focus. Double-check heaters and thermometers, plan for power outages, and manage indoor humidity; some species brumate now.

spring.reptile: Raise basking temps in steps and lengthen the photoperiod. Restore feeding as appetite returns, and check condition and weight before pairing breeders.
spring.arthropod: Activity and appetite rise, so feed more often. Adjust humidity to spring conditions and watch growing specimens closely for molt signs.
spring.bird: Breeding/molt season and longer days raise activity. Boost protein and calcium, and offer more fresh water and bathing to keep plumage in shape.
spring.amphibian: Activity resumes as water and air warm. Increase feeding gradually, check water quality often, and provide breeding cues for species that need them.
spring.mammal: Activity and appetite recover. Shedding begins, so brush more often and ease off bedding insulation as spring temperatures rise.
spring.general: Day-night swings are wide: keep night heating on while raising daytime temps in steps. Lengthen the photoperiod and normalise feeding as appetite returns.

summer.reptile: Overheating is the top risk. Check peak enclosure temps often, avoid direct AC drafts and sharp temperature swings, and provide ample water, shade, and hides.
summer.arthropod: Airflow and moisture matter in heat. Mist to hold humidity but avoid stagnant hot air, and prevent overheating from direct sun or a sealed enclosure.
summer.bird: Watch for heat stress. Keep birds in well-ventilated shade, refresh water often, and position them so AC drafts don't hit them directly.
summer.amphibian: Heat is deadly to amphibians. Use cooling fans, ice packs, or relocate the tank to stop water temps rising, and manage water quality and oxygen too.
summer.mammal: They tolerate heat and dehydration poorly. Provide ventilation and shade, offer cool substrate or tile, and keep fresh water always available.
summer.general: Overheating is the top risk. Check peak enclosure temps often, avoid direct AC drafts and sharp temperature swings, and ensure airflow and ample water.

autumn.reptile: Shortening daylight shifts appetite and activity. Species that brumate should plan a vet check and fasting schedule; others should pre-check heating gear.
autumn.arthropod: Cooling temps reduce appetite and activity. Adjust feeding and begin heating, while preparing humidity control for winter dryness.
autumn.bird: Falling daylight wraps up the molt. Reinforce nutrition, check nighttime heating, and consider supplemental light if days are short.
autumn.amphibian: Activity drops as water and air cool. Reduce feeding, check the heater, and prepare for dryness from indoor heating.
autumn.mammal: Appetite may rise ahead of winter. Add bedding insulation, block drafts, and watch respiratory and immune health through the change of season.
autumn.general: Shortening daylight shifts appetite and activity. Pre-check winter heating gear, and plan a brumation prep schedule for species that need one.

winter.reptile: Heating, outage readiness, and dryness are key. Double-check heaters and thermometers and mist to add humidity. Some species brumate now.
winter.arthropod: Heating and dryness prevention matter. Indoor heating drops humidity, so mist more, and judge whether cold-driven dips in activity and appetite are normal.
winter.bird: Block drafts and sharp temperature swings, and reinforce nighttime heating. Counter heater dryness with humidity and add light if days are short.
winter.amphibian: Check the heater to stop water temps falling, and watch for evaporation and water-quality shifts from indoor dryness. Prepare for outages too.
winter.mammal: Warmth and draft-blocking come first. Hold a steady temperature and add bedding, and watch for dryness and poor ventilation from heating.
winter.general: Heating, outage readiness, and dryness are key. Double-check heaters and thermometers, plan for power outages, and replace humidity lost to heating.

brumationNote.text: If your reptile brumates in winter, plan its schedule and phases ahead.

overview.title: All four seasons at a glance
overview.lead: Key per-season points for {{category}}. The current season is highlighted.

lighting.title: Lighting & Photoperiod
lighting.subtitle: Day length (photoperiod) and UVB on-time drive appetite, breeding, and bone health. Set your lights-on and lights-off times and we'll compare the result to the season-appropriate target for your category. (Northern hemisphere baseline.)

verdictHint.low: The day is shorter than recommended. Extend the photoperiod to lift appetite and activity.
verdictHint.ok: You're within the recommended range. Re-check as the season changes.
verdictHint.high: The day is longer than recommended. Shorten the photoperiod to avoid stress and chronic egg-laying.

uvb.title: UVB guidance
uvb.decay: A UVB lamp's UV output fades long before its visible light does. Replace it on schedule. (Track replacement cycles alongside your other gear.)
uvb.reptile: Diurnal reptiles need UVB for D3 synthesis and calcium metabolism. Even nocturnal geckos benefit from low-level UVB. Position it so the UV index reaches the basking spot.
uvb.bird: Birds benefit from full-spectrum (UVA/UVB) lighting for natural behaviour and vitamin D3. Use an avian UV lamp where indoor housing lacks natural sunlight.
uvb.amphibian: Most amphibians benefit from low-level UVB but avoid strong direct light. Prioritise ample hides and shade first.
uvb.arthropod: Most arthropods need no UVB. Provide a gentle day/night cue rather than strong direct light.
uvb.mammal: Most small mammals are nocturnal/crepuscular and need no dedicated UVB. A stable day/night rhythm matters more than bright light.

lighting.disclaimer: A general northern-hemisphere (Korea / Japan) guide. Exact photoperiod and UVB intensity vary by species, individual, and origin latitude, so check your species' references and expert guidance too.

bcs.title: Body score
bcs.subtitle: Weight alone misses obesity and wasting. Log a 1–5 body condition score (BCS) over time and watch the trend.
bcs.intro: Body condition score complements weight tracking. Two animals at the same weight can be in very different shape, so noting what you see and feel helps catch obesity or wasting earlier.

trend.label: Score trend
trend.aria: Body condition score trend, {{count}} data points

bcs.scaleDesc: The 1–5 scale tailored to this species. Your most recent score is highlighted.
bcs.statusLegend: 1–2 is underweight, 3 is ideal, 4–5 is overweight. If the score stays skewed one way, consult a vet.

reptile.1: Pelvis, ribs and spine clearly visible with a thin tail. Emaciated.
reptile.2: Ribs and spine palpable, tail lacks fill. Slightly thin.
reptile.3: Ideal. Full musculature, ribs lightly palpable, tail smooth.
reptile.4: Ribs hard to feel; fat starting at the tail base and flanks. Slightly overweight.
reptile.5: Obese. Prominent fat pads, thick tail, fat in the armpits.

amphibian.1: Pelvis and backbone prominent with sunken flanks. Wasting.
amphibian.2: Dorsal line a bit angular and underfilled. Slightly thin.
amphibian.3: Ideal. A smooth curve from head through the body.
amphibian.4: Rounded body with slightly bulging flanks. Slightly overweight.
amphibian.5: Obese. Distended lymph sacs and bulging flanks.

arthropod.1: Abdomen smaller than the prosoma and wrinkled. Suspect dehydration or undernutrition.
arthropod.2: Abdomen similar to or slightly smaller than the prosoma. Needs feeding up.
arthropod.3: Ideal. Abdomen moderately larger than the prosoma and taut.
arthropod.4: Abdomen swollen and glossy. Somewhat overfed.
arthropod.5: Abdomen overdistended with taut skin. Rupture risk — cut back feeding.

bird.1: Keel sharply protruding with almost no breast muscle. Wasting.
bird.2: Keel prominent, muscle shallow. Slightly thin.
bird.3: Ideal. Muscle fills out either side of the keel into a gentle curve.
bird.4: Keel buried in muscle, chest rounded. Slightly overweight.
bird.5: Obese. Keel fully covered with fat over the abdomen.

mammal.1: Ribs, spine and pelvis obvious with a deeply tucked waist. Wasting.
mammal.2: Ribs easily felt with a marked waist. Slightly thin.
mammal.3: Ideal. Ribs lightly palpable with a soft waistline.
mammal.4: Ribs hard to feel and the waist disappears. Slightly overweight.
mammal.5: Obese. Ribs buried under heavy fat with a sagging abdomen.

unknown.1: Skeleton prominent with almost no flesh. Wasting.
unknown.2: Bones easily felt and underfilled. Slightly thin.
unknown.3: Ideal. Bones lightly palpable with a balanced shape.
unknown.4: Bones hard to feel and flesh building up. Slightly overweight.
unknown.5: Obese. Bones barely palpable under heavy fat.

bcs.scoreHint: Pick 1–5 based on what you see and feel.
bcs.emptyDesc: Log your first body score to start tracking changes.
bcs.vetNote: If an under- or overweight score persists, consult a vet. Body condition score is most accurate alongside weight tracking.
